config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. In my view, society matters a lot, but individual rights should not be treated as secondary by default, because that opens the door to abuse by governments or majorities. From my perspective, a healthy society is built by protecting personal freedom, dignity, and due process first, then balancing those with the common good when there is a real need. Collective interests can justify limits sometimes, but only narrowly and with clear accountability.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the individual, because that can too easily become an excuse for suppressing people in the name of harmony or efficiency. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why many people emphasize collective stability, since social order and shared responsibility are deeply valued and can genuinely protect ordinary people. But I also think individual rights need real protection, especially when the majority or the state claims to know what is best, because without limits the individual becomes too vulnerable. The right balance is not “society first” in every case, but a serious case-by-case judgment where collective needs are real and individual rights are not treated as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. In Indonesia, we know how easily “the common good” can be used to pressure dissent, silence minorities, or excuse abuse, so I am very wary of any rule that puts society first by default. Social needs do matter, especially in crises, but they should justify only limited and accountable restrictions, not a blank check to override basic rights. A strong society is not one that crushes individuals for harmony, but one that protects people well enough that they can trust the collective in the first place.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why many people value stability, order, and collective responsibility, because those can reduce chaos and protect ordinary people, but I also know how easily “society first” can become a justification for silencing criticism or ignoring personal dignity. The strongest society is not one that asks individuals to sacrifice everything, but one that sets clear limits on power and only restricts rights when there is a real, necessary, and accountable public reason. If individual rights are treated as disposable, then the so-called social interest can quickly become whatever the powerful say it is.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support the idea that society should take priority over individual rights by default. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to harmony, order, or the common good can be used to pressure minorities and dismiss uncomfortable voices, so I am cautious of any principle that makes individual rights conditional on majority preference. Society does matter, especially in emergencies, but its interests should only limit rights in narrow, transparent, and accountable ways. If rights are not protected first, then “society” can become just a polite word for power.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand the strong appeal of stability, order, and collective responsibility, because social harmony can genuinely protect ordinary people and reduce conflict. But I have also seen how easily “for the good of society” can be used to silence criticism or excuse unfair treatment, so I do not trust that phrase unless there are clear limits and accountability. A society is strongest when it protects individuals first enough that collective interests are pursued through consent and law, not by treating rights as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
